serving.description: 1 whole crab (~500 g edible + shell weight)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving is one whole crab weighing approximately 500 grams including shell, yielding roughly 150–200 g of edible crab meat after shelling.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15139); supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood dish data for sodium and fat adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base macros derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139: energy 97 kcal, protein 20.1 g, fat 1.7 g, carbohydrate 0 g per 100 g edible portion). Stir-frying with oil, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and ginger adds fat (~3–4 g/100 g edible), sodium (~250–350 mg/100 g), and minor carbohydrate (~2–3 g/100 g) from sauce ingredients. Final per-100 g values represent the edible cooked crab portion inclusive of stir-fry sauce coating, estimated by component addition consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD methodology for stir-fried seafood dishes. Values are per 100 g of edible portion (meat + sauce, shell excluded).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate for cooked crustacean / seafood protein dish
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried whole crab. Cooked crustaceans and high-protein seafood dishes are consistently classified as very low GI (GI ≤ 10) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, as protein and fat dominate the macronutrient profile with negligible glycemic carbohydrate. GI estimated at 5 (analogous to cooked shrimp/prawn and other stir-fried seafood protein dishes). Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated on ~8.4 g available carbohydrate in edible portion: GL = (5 × 8.4) / 100 = 0.42, rounded to 0.8 to account for sauce carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~480 mg/100 g edible portion; ~720–960 mg per typical restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation, as the soy sauce and oyster sauce base contributes substantial sodium that can approach 30–40% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2000 mg) in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~5) and low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g); high protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried crab is a suitable protein-rich, low-carbohydrate option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, with a negligible glycemic load per serving; however, the sodium-rich sauce warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (~78 mg/100 g), elevated sodium from sauce, but beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein, which support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from stir-fry sauces and moderate dietary cholesterol mean that individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer reduced-sauce preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Whole Crab is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean tze char (zi char) seafood dish consisting of one whole live or fresh crab — typically mud crab (Scylla serrata) or flower crab (Portunus pelagicus) — cooked in a hot wok with garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and chilli. The dish is characterised by its intensely savoury, umami-rich sauce that coats the crab shell and meat, with a slightly caramelised wok-hei aroma from high-heat stir-frying. The crab meat is tender, sweet, and succulent, contrasting with the bold, salty-savoury sauce. A single restaurant serving typically weighs approximately 500 grams including shell, yielding 150–200 g of edible meat. Nutritionally, the edible portion is high in lean protein (~18.5 g/100 g), low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g/100 g), and provides significant vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus. The dish has a very low glycemic index (~5) making it suitable for blood glucose management, but its sodium content (~480 mg/100 g edible) from soy and oyster sauces warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is a staple of Malaysian Chinese tze char restaurants and is widely available across Singapore hawker centres and seafood restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir fry crab?
Can diabetics eat stir fried crab?
Is crab stir fry high in cholesterol?
Stir fried crab for weight loss

confidence_reason: No exact stir-fried whole crab entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the time of processing. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139) with stir-fry sauce additions estimated using component methodology consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood dish patterns. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature for high-protein crustacean dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base crab nutrient data is from a high-quality primary source and the sauce adjustment methodology is systematic and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of edible portion (crab meat + sauce coating; shell excluded). The 500 g restaurant serving weight includes shell; edible yield is approximately 150–200 g. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab entry and is not available from the adapted sources used. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked crab, and the stir-fry oils used (vegetable oil) in standard Malaysian tze char preparation do not contribute measurable trans fat. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish is commonly prepared in tze char restaurants that may use non-halal ingredients or shared cooking equipment; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 5. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous cooked crustacean data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) AJCN international GI tables; no direct measurement for stir-fried whole crab exists in the approved GI literature. 6. Sodium value (~480 mg/100 g edible) is an estimate incorporating soy sauce and oyster sauce contributions; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant recipe. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab data and are marked as adapted. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database.
